results.possibleIdentity: Larry Hahn is a multidisciplinary artist, dancer, set designer, and builder whose career has spanned over five decades, beginning in the late 1960s or early 1970s. He was a prominent figure in the New York contemporary dance scene, most notably as a long-term member of Doug Varone and Dancers (1988–2003) and a collaborator with choreographers such as Margaret Jenkins, Gus Solomons Jr., Senta Driver, and Mark Morris. Parallel to his performance career, Hahn developed a specialized practice in designing and constructing sculptural light forms, theatrical sets, and furniture. This technical and artistic crossover align with the collaborative spirit of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In 1996, he received a New York Dance and Performance Award (Bessie) for his achievements in dance. Hahn is currently based in Ontario, Canada, where he continues to work in photography and stage design, often participating in interdisciplinary projects that bridge art, science, and technology, such as those hosted by Inter Arts Matrix.

results.possibleIdentity: Bruce Alan MacCurdy (c. 1944–1987) was an American filmmaker, animator, and educator active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He taught filmmaking and animation for ten years at Syracuse University, where he mentored future director Henry Selick. MacCurdy was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, contributing to their collaborative projects between artists and engineers. His work, such as the seven-minute color film 'Willow' and the animated film 'Flipping,' was included in E.A.T. exhibition tours and referenced in key animation literature like Kit Laybourne’s 'The Animation Book.' He was also a close friend and collaborator of cartoonist Vaughn Bodé, with whom he worked on scripts for animated features such as 'The Amorous Adventures of Puck.' MacCurdy resided in Syracuse starting in 1962 and later moved to New Woodstock, New York, where he worked as a self-employed filmmaker producing content for public television and Colgate University until his death in 1987.

results.possibleIdentity: C. Alexander Brown was a multi-disciplinary artist, songwriter, poet, and activist active in Canada during the 1960s and 1970s. Based in Montreal and Ottawa, he was involved in several artistic and social circles that crossed into technical and organizational fields, aligning with the ethos of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In the 1960s, he was an activist associated with West Indian radical thought in Montreal, writing pieces like 'Exile from a Warm Land' (1961) for Sir George Williams University's student newspaper, The Georgian. He was also a correspondent for Oxfam's Montreal office in 1962, focusing on welfare and organization expertise. In 1965, he co-authored a technical brief on automobile safety for the Canadian government alongside Heward Grafftey and Rheal Casavant, showcasing an early interest in the intersection of engineering and public service. As an artist, he corresponded with prominent Canadian artist Greg Curnoe in 1966 and later sent exhibition notices to journalist Colin Legum in 1974. His musical career included songwriting, with his work 'Change in the Weather' copyrighted in 1973; he had professional ties to Folkways Records, Columbia Records, and the Canadian music industry publication RPM. He also contributed poetry and prose, such as the piece 'SUGARBUSH' (1972), to alternative and student publications like The Charlatan.
